openingMessage: New week, new standard on the board — Confined Space. This is the one where there's no such thing as 'probably fine.' You test the atmosphere before anyone goes in, you keep the monitor running the whole time they're inside, and nobody enters without an attendant standing by and a way to get them out. If you've done a hundred tank entries, you're exactly the hand I need slowing down to coach the guy who's done one. That's what this week is really about — the standard, and passing it on.
leadershipThought: Confined space is where this company's discipline either shows up or it doesn't — there's no hiding it. The atmosphere is either tested or it isn't. The attendant is either posted at the entry or he's thirty feet away at the truck. The rescue plan is either staged or it's a hope. We've already set the bar: Jacob's SDU entry done fully under air with testing before, during, and after; Edward stopping a job when benzene climbed even though the crew was already on air; Alex staging primary and secondary rescue before a single man broke containment. That's the standard. But this week is 'Coaching Others' for a reason — knowing the rule yourself isn't enough anymore. The measure of a leader on this crew is whether the newest hand knows it too, because you took the time to walk him through it. Protect the man next to you by making sure he knows what you know.
opsContent:
Active Jobs (2026-07-13):
Monday assignments post in Breakroom at start of shift.
Carry-Forward:
SM Energy #112 — 6,000 ppm H2S / 2% CO2 reported Friday 7/10 (Michael Hutt). Confirm current readings before any entry or first break.
Driving — two near-misses flagged Friday (swerving driver on Jal 128; service truck drifting into lane at 75 mph). Heads-up on the drive in.
exxonMoments:
SDU 1602A — confined space entry by the book [Jacob Hernandez] (Confined Space)
Gun barrel cleaned, crew under air for the valve change and hatch repair, tank entry made under air, atmospheric testing done before, during, and after. Co Rep released the crew. Every box checked — this is the Week 5 standard.

Acted on the reading, didn't just take it — Turner E2 1202A [Edward Carrillo] (Confined Space)
Benzene climbed above the exposure limit even with the crew already under air. He stopped work, cleared the area, ran a second test confirming safe conditions, then resumed — and every party was receptive when asked to stand down. Real-time monitoring means acting on the numbers, not just reading them.

Rescue staged before anyone entered — H2S separator [Alex Clubb] (Confined Space)
Safety meeting with the Superintendent and PSMS on site, one person under air for the break, himself as primary rescue and Justin as secondary. AGT confirmed zeros on all headers — and only then did the crew proceed without air. The rescue plan was staged before a single man broke containment.

Leak found before the break — supplied air as the default [Stefanie Kirschke] (Confined Space)
A leak was identified before the line was broken and reported to the company rep. Rather than wait to confirm exposure levels, the crew went on supplied air for first break, with continuous monitoring throughout. Find a leak before you break containment, and supplied air is the default — not the fallback.

Tested zeros before trusting them — Newmont Palomino [Justin Fontenot] (Confined Space)
First break made under air. Tested zeros. Only then did work continue without air. Test, confirm, proceed — the atmosphere inside is the floor at the start, never the ceiling for the whole job.

fieldNews.headline: Jacob Hernandez — SDU 1602A confined space, every box checked
fieldNews.detail: Gun barrel cleaned, tank entry under air, atmospheric testing before/during/after, Co Rep released the crew.

fieldNews.headline: Edward Carrillo — stopped the job when benzene climbed under air
fieldNews.detail: Turner E2 1202A — above exposure limit while on air; cleared, retested, resumed only when confirmed safe.

fieldNews.headline: Alex Clubb — primary and secondary rescue staged before the break
fieldNews.detail: H2S separator — one under air, AGT zeros on all headers confirmed before proceeding without air.

fieldNews.headline: Stefanie Kirschke — supplied air as the default when a leak showed up
fieldNews.detail: Leak identified before the break, reported to the company rep, crew placed on air with continuous monitoring.

fieldNews.headline: Justin Fontenot — tested zeros before working without air
fieldNews.detail: Newmont Palomino — first break under air, confirmed zeros, then continued. Test, confirm, proceed.

fieldNews.headline: SM Energy #112 — 6,000 ppm H2S / 2% CO2 (carry-forward from 7/10)
fieldNews.detail: Michael Hutt reported it Friday. Confirm current readings before any entry or first break this week.

fieldNews.headline: Two driving near-misses flagged Friday — stay heads-up on the road
fieldNews.detail: Jordan Lemmons (swerving driver, Jal 128) and Alex Clubb (service truck into his lane at 75 mph). The drive is part of the job.

todaysChallenge: Before anyone enters a confined space this week, say the five out loud as a crew: atmosphere tested, monitor running, attendant posted and staying put, rescue plan staged, permit signed. If you can't check all five, nobody goes in. Today's coaching rep — walk one newer hand through why each of the five exists. Don't just give him the rule. Give him the reason.
todaysChallengeSubtext: Week 5 is Confined Space, and the theme is Coaching Others. The hands who've done this a thousand times are exactly the ones who make the difference for the guy who hasn't. Slow down. Pass it on.

compliance.footerNote: Week 4 final — update with Week 5 counts Monday AM
